raw_response:
{
  "communicative_function": "contrasting two passions to evoke a moral hierarchy of self-mastery through figurative language",
  "implicit_claims": [
    "anger involves a visible withdrawal from reason accompanied by grief-like contraction",
    "lust reveals a deeper failure of self-control and masculine virtue than anger does",
    "moral character can be diagnosed from the manner in which one yields to pleasure or pain"
  ],
  "coherence_assessment": "internally consistent within a Stoic-style taxonomy of passions that ranks failures of reason by degree of perceived weakness",
  "conditions_for_usefulness": "the reader accepts an ethical framework that treats self-command as paramount and is open to literary rather than empirical description of emotion",
  "conditions_for_misleading": "the passage is read as literal psychological or gendered fact rather than stylized moral imagery, or is invoked to justify contempt for certain forms of desire"
}
